"let pstate = offset_of!(user_pt_regs, pstate) + kreg_off; let id = arm64_core_reg_id!(KVM_REG_SIZE_U64, pstate); self.fd.set_one_reg(id, &PSTATE_FAULT_BITS_64.to_le_bytes())". `test_configure_vcpu` (in vcpu.rs) asserts the error carries register 0x6030000000100042 with value "0x3c5".to_string() — confirming PSTATE_FAULT_BITS_64 == 0x3c5. 0x3c5 = 0b11_1100_0101: M[3:0]=0b0101 = EL1h, and bits 9/8/7/6 (D/A/I/F) all set = 0x3c0 masked. The DAIF-masked EL1h interpretation is exactly correct.

In `setup_boot_regs` (index 0 only): "let pc = offset_of!(user_pt_regs, pc) + kreg_off; ... self.fd.set_one_reg(id, &boot_ip.to_le_bytes())", where boot_ip = kernel_entry_point.entry_addr.raw_value() (from `configure`). mod.rs: "pub fn get_kernel_start() -> u64 { layout::SYSTEM_MEM_START + layout::SYSTEM_MEM_SIZE }". layout.rs: "pub const DRAM_MEM_START: u64 = 0x8000_0000;", "pub const SYSTEM_MEM_START: u64 = DRAM_MEM_START;", "pub const SYSTEM_MEM_SIZE: u64 = 0x20_0000;". In `setup_boot_regs`: "let regs0 = offset_of!(user_pt_regs, regs) + kreg_off; ... let fdt_addr = get_fdt_addr(mem); self.fd.set_one_reg(id, &fdt_addr.to_le_bytes())" — regs0 is the first GP register x0; comment: "We are choosing to place it the end of DRAM. fact: arm64 PE/Linux Image header is 64 bytes: code0/code1 (branch to stext), u64 text_offset, u64 image_size, u64 flags (bit0 endian, bits1-2 page size, bit3 placement), reserved, u32 magic = 0x644d5241 ('ARM\x64'), u32 PE-COFF offset. Boot contract: x0 = DTB phys addr, x1=x2=x3=0, MMU off, DAIF masked, EL2 (recommended) or non-secure EL1, DTB 8-byte aligned and <= 2 MiB.
evidence:
Fetched https://www.kernel.org/doc/Documentation/arm64/booting.txt (HTTP 200). Every detail matches exactly.

HEADER (source: "The decompressed kernel image contains a 64-byte header as follows:"):
  u32 code0;          /* Executable code */
  u32 code1;          /* Executable code */
  u64 text_offset;    /* Image load offset, little endian */
  u64 image_size;     /* Effective Image size, little endian */
  u64 flags;          /* kernel flags, little endian */
  u64 res2 = 0;       /* reserved */
  u64 res3 = 0;       /* reserved */
  u64 res4 = 0;       /* reserved */
  u32 magic = 0x644d5241;  /* Magic number, little endian, "ARM\x64" */
  u32 res5;           /* reserved (used for PE COFF offset) */

Field offsets resolve to: code0=0, code1=4, text_offset=8, image_size=16, flags=24, res2=32, res3=40, res4=48, magic=56, res5=60 -> 64 bytes total, confirming the "64-byte header" claim.

code0/code1 -> stext: "code0/code1 are responsible for branching to stext."

flags bits (verbatim): "Bit 0: Kernel endianness. 1 if BE, 0 if LE." / "Bit 1-2: Kernel Page size." / "Bit 3: Kernel physical placement" / "Bits 4-63: Reserved." Matches claim's "bit0 endian, bits1-2 page size, bit3 placement."

magic: 'u32 magic = 0x644d5241; /* Magic number, little endian, "ARM\x64" */' -> exact match for 0x644d5241 and "ARM\x64".

PE-COFF offset: res5 "/* reserved (used for PE COFF offset) */" and "res5 is an offset to the PE header" -> confirms "u32 PE-COFF offset."

BOOT CONTRACT:
- "x0 = physical address of device tree blob (dtb) in system RAM." / "x1 = 0" / "x2 = 0" / "x3 = 0" -> matches x0=DTB phys, x1=x2=x3=0.
- "The MMU must be off." -> MMU off.
- "All forms of interrupts must be masked in PSTATE.DAIF (Debug, SError, IRQ and FIQ)." -> DAIF masked.
- "The CPU must be in either EL2 (RECOMMENDED in order to have access to the virtualisation extensions) or non-secure EL1." -> EL2 (recommended) or non-secure EL1.
- "The device tree blob (dtb) must be placed on an 8-byte boundary and must not exceed 2 megabytes in size." -> DTB 8-byte aligned and <= 2 MiB.

(Note: the source has three reserved u64 fields res2/res3/res4 between flags and magic; the claim's singular "reserved" is a correct, non-misleading summary of that reserved block and does not assert a wrong count of any named register/field.)

fact: Linux x86 64-bit boot protocol requires entry in 64-bit mode with paging enabled, a GDT with flat 4G __BOOT_CS(0x10)/__BOOT_DS(0x18), interrupts disabled, and %rsi = base address of struct boot_params. (Firecracker uses selectors 0x08/0x10 but identical flat descriptors; the kernel must reload its own GDT.)
evidence:
kernel.org section 1.14 "64-bit Boot Protocol" supports every detail verbatim: "At entry, the CPU must be in 64-bit mode with paging enabled. ... a GDT must be loaded with the descriptors for selectors __BOOT_CS(0x10) and __BOOT_DS(0x18); both descriptors must be 4G flat segment; __BOOT_CS must have execute/read permission, and __BOOT_DS must have read/write permission; CS must be __BOOT_CS and DS, ES, SS must be __BOOT_DS; interrupt must be disabled; %rsi must hold the base address of the struct boot_params." Register/selector exactness: __BOOT_CS=0x10, __BOOT_DS=0x18, %rsi (correctly the 64-bit register, not the %esi of the 32-bit protocol in section 1.13); the fact also correctly omits the %ebp/%edi/%ebx=0 requirement that applies only to the 32-bit protocol.

Firecracker parenthetical confirmed against src/vmm/src/arch/x86_64/regs.rs and gdt.rs (main branch). regs.rs configure_segments_and_sregs (LinuxBoot): `let code_seg = kvm_segment_from_gdt(gdt_table[1], 1);` and `let data_seg = kvm_segment_from_gdt(gdt_table[2], 2);`, with gdt.rs `selector: u16::from(table_index * 8)` -> CS selector = 1*8 = 0x08, DS/ES/FS/GS/SS selector = 2*8 = 0x10 (test asserts `assert_eq!(0x10, sregs.es.selector);`). So Firecracker uses 0x08/0x10, exactly as claimed. The descriptors are flat 4G: LinuxBoot GDT entries `gdt_entry(0xa09b, 0, 0xfffff)` (CODE) and `gdt_entry(0xc093, 0, 0xfffff)` (DATA), base 0, limit scaled to 0xFFFF_FFFF via the G flag (get_limit: `(limit << 12) | 0xFFF`), matching the spec's "4G flat segment". The note that the kernel reloads its own GDT is consistent with the handoff design. All register names, counts, selector/field offsets, and addresses are exactly right.

Every detail of the fact is exactly supported, with correct scope (the 64-bit protocol, not the 32-bit one), and no overclaim.

MAIN FACT — kernel.org "1.14. 64-bit Boot Protocol" (https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/latest/arch/x86/boot.html), quoted verbatim:
"At entry, the CPU must be in 64-bit mode with paging enabled. The range with setup_header.init_size from start address of loaded kernel and zero page and command line buffer get ident mapping; a GDT must be loaded with the descriptors for selectors __BOOT_CS(0x10) and __BOOT_DS(0x18); both descriptors must be 4G flat segment; __BOOT_CS must have execute/read permission, and __BOOT_DS must have read/write permission; CS must be __BOOT_CS and DS, ES, SS must be __BOOT_DS; interrupt must be disabled; %rsi must hold the base address of the struct boot_params."

Mapping each claimed element:
- "entry in 64-bit mode with paging enabled" -> "the CPU must be in 64-bit mode with paging enabled" (CONFIRMED).
- "a GDT with flat 4G __BOOT_CS(0x10)/__BOOT_DS(0x18)" -> "a GDT must be loaded with the descriptors for selectors __BOOT_CS(0x10) and __BOOT_DS(0x18); both descriptors must be 4G flat segment" (CONFIRMED, exact selector values).
- "interrupts disabled" -> "interrupt must be disabled" (CONFIRMED).
- "%rsi = base address of struct boot_params" -> "%rsi must hold the base address of the struct boot_params" (CONFIRMED).

SCOPE-EXACTNESS check: The same selector values __BOOT_CS(0x10)/__BOOT_DS(0x18) ALSO appear in the adjacent "1.13. 32-bit Boot Protocol" section, but that one says "the CPU must be in 32-bit protected mode with paging disabled" and "%esi must hold the base address". The fact correctly pairs the selectors with the 64-bit attributes (64-bit mode, paging ENABLED, %rsi), so the 64-bit-vs-32-bit scope is exactly right — no cross-section conflation.

PARENTHETICAL (Firecracker) — verified against Firecracker source src/vmm/src/arch/x86_64/regs.rs and gdt.rs (github.com/firecracker-microvm/firecracker, main):
- "Firecracker uses selectors 0x08/0x10": In configure_segments_and_sregs, code_seg = kvm_segment_from_gdt(gdt_table[1], 1) and data_seg = kvm_segment_from_gdt(gdt_table[2], 2). In gdt.rs, kvm_segment_from_gdt sets selector = table_index * 8, so CS selector = 0x08 and DS/ES/FS/GS/SS selector = 0x10; the unit test asserts assert_eq!(0x10, sregs.es.selector). (CONFIRMED — CS=0x08, data=0x10, i.e. GDT indices 1/2 instead of the kernel's 2/3.)
- "identical flat descriptors": Firecracker's LinuxBoot GDT is [NULL, gdt_entry(0xa09b,0,0xfffff) CODE, gdt_entry(0xc093,0,0xfffff) DATA, ...]; with the G flag set the 0xfffff limit scales to 0xffff_ffff (4G), base 0, CODE type 0xb (execute/read) and DATA type 0x3 (read/write) — i.e. the same 4G-flat exec/read and read/write descriptors the kernel.org spec mandates, merely at different GDT indices. (CONFIRMED.)
- "the kernel must reload its own GDT": correct and necessary, since Firecracker hands off CS=0x08/DS=0x10 whereas the kernel's internal __BOOT_CS/__BOOT_DS are 0x10/0x18; the kernel's startup_64 path installs its own GDT.

No detail in the fact is off and there is no version/scope overclaim, so the verdict is confirmed.

fact: x86_64 integer argument registers in order = rdi, rsi, rdx, rcx, r8, r9; integer return = rax then rdx; %al carries the vector-register count for variadic calls.
source: AMD64 psABI 1.0 §3.2.3 Parameter Passing / Returning of Values
evidence:
Source: AMD64 System V psABI Version 1.0, March 12, 2025 (downloaded directly from gitlab.com/x86-psABIs/x86-64-ABI build artifact abi.pdf), §3.2.3 "Parameter Passing". All three sub-claims match verbatim.

(1) Integer argument registers in order — §3.2.3 "Passing", item 2: "If the class is INTEGER, the next available register of the sequence %rdi, %rsi, %rdx, %rcx, %r8 and %r9 is used." This is exactly rdi, rsi, rdx, rcx, r8, r9 (6 registers, correct order).

(2) Integer return = rax then rdx — §3.2.3 "Returning of Values", item 3: "If the class is INTEGER, the next available register of the sequence %rax, %rdx is used." Exact: rax then rdx.

(3) %al carries the vector-register count for variadic calls — §3.2.3 footnote 19: "%al is used to indicate the number of vector arguments passed to a function requiring a variable number of arguments." Footnote 22: "Note that the rest of %rax is undefined, only the contents of %al is defined."

source: AMD64 psABI 1.0 §3.2.2; rustc book platform-support/x86_64-unknown-none ('extern "C" ... without red zones'; 'does not use any vector or floating-point registers')
evidence:
All three sub-claims are exactly supported, verified against the directly-fetched authoritative sources. Register/count/offset/address details are precise.

(1) 16-byte alignment before `call` — AMD64 psABI 1.0 §3.2.2 "The Stack Frame" (PDF downloaded from gitlab.com/x86-psABIs/x86-64-ABI, edition "AMD64 ABI 1.0 - March 12, 2025"): "The end of the input argument area shall be aligned on a 16 (32 or 64, if __m256 or __m512 is passed on stack) byte boundary. In other words, the stack needs to be 16 (32 or 64) byte aligned immediately before the call instruction is executed." It further states: "immediately after the return address has been pushed, %rsp points to the return address, and the value of (%rsp + 8) is a multiple of 16 (32 or 64)." The FACT's baseline of 16-byte alignment is correct (32/64 only apply when __m256/__m512 are stack-passed).

(2) 128-byte red zone, not modified by signal/interrupt handlers — Same §3.2.2: "The 128-byte area beyond the location pointed to by %rsp is considered to be reserved and shall not be modified by signal or interrupt handlers. Therefore, functions may use this area for temporary data that is not needed across function calls. In particular, leaf functions may use this area for their entire stack frame... This area is known as the red zone." Figure 3.3 in the same section depicts the red zone spanning from 0(%rsp) down to -128(%rsp). Exact: 128 bytes, below %rsp, signal/interrupt handlers must not modify. Confirmed.

(3) x86_64-unknown-none, Tier 2, no SSE/AVX, red zone disabled — rustc book platform-support/x86_64-unknown-none (doc.rust-lang.org, fetched HTTP 200): "Tier: 2". "By default, Rust code generated for this target does not use any vector or floating-point registers (e.g. SSE, AVX)." And: "On x86_64-unknown-none, extern \"C\" uses the standard System V calling convention, without red zones." All three details (Tier 2; no vector/FP registers; red zones disabled) match verbatim.

Every element verified against the fetched authoritative sources with no overclaim or scope error.

AMD64 psABI (PDF downloaded from gitlab.com/x86-psABIs/x86-64-ABI; document header "AMD64 ABI 1.0 — March 12, 2025"), §3.2.2 "The Stack Frame":

- Red zone: "The 128-byte area beyond the location pointed to by %rsp is considered to be reserved and shall not be modified by signal or interrupt handlers. ... This area is known as the red zone." Section number §3.2.2 and version 1.0 are correct.

- "On x86_64-unknown-none, extern \"C\" uses the standard System V calling convention, without red zones." => "disables the red zone" confirmed; quoted snippet matches.

fact: aarch64 SIMD/FP: v0-v7 = params/results, v8-v15 callee-saved but ONLY the bottom 64 bits, v16-v31 caller-saved; NZCV flags undefined across a public interface; FPSR not preserved.
source: AAPCS64 §SIMD and Floating-Point registers: 'Registers v8-v15 are Callee-saved ... only the bottom 64 bits ... need to be Callee-saved'
evidence:
Verified against the live ARM AAPCS64 source (raw RST from github.com/ARM-software/abi-aa, file aapcs64/aapcs64.rst). Every register name, count, and the flag/status-register details are exact:

1. v0-v7 = params/results: "The first eight registers, v0-v7, are used to pass argument values into a subroutine and to return result values from a function. They may also be used to hold intermediate values within a routine..."

2. v8-v15 callee-saved but ONLY bottom 64 bits; v16-v31 caller-saved: "Registers v8-v15 are `Callee-saved`_ and the remaining registers (v0-v7, v16-v31) are `Caller-saved`_. Additionally, only the bottom 64 bits of each value stored in v8-v15 need to be `Callee-saved`_ [#aapcs64-f7]_; it is the responsibility of the caller to preserve larger values." (This matches the cited SOURCE quote exactly.)

3. NZCV flags undefined across a public interface: "The NZCV register is a global condition flag register with the following properties: - The N, Z, C and V flags are undefined on entry to and return from a public interface."

4. FPSR not preserved: "The FPSR is a status register that holds the cumulative exception bits of the floating-point unit. It contains the fields IDC, IXC, UFC, OFC, DZC, IOC and QC. These fields are not preserved across a public interface and may have any value on entry to a subroutine."

The 32-register count (v0-v31) is also confirmed: "The Arm 64-bit architecture also has a further thirty-two registers, v0-v31, which can be used by SIMD and Floating-Point operations."

Only trivial non-material note: the actual section heading is "SIMD and Floating-Point registers" (lowercase "registers"), whereas the SOURCE citation rendered it "...Registers". No factual detail is affected.

fact: aarch64 stack alignment: SP mod 16 = 0 (quad-word aligned) at all public interfaces; the frame record is two 64-bit values (FP,LR) on the stack.
source: AAPCS64 Universal stack constraints: 'SP mod 16 = 0. The stack must be quad-word aligned'
evidence:
Fetched AAPCS64 source directly: raw.githubusercontent.com/ARM-software/abi-aa/main/aapcs64/aapcs64.rst.

Claim 1 (SP mod 16 = 0, quad-word aligned, at public interfaces) — CONFIRMED. The "Universal stack constraints" section states verbatim: "Additionally, at any point at which memory is accessed via SP, the hardware requires that - SP mod 16 = 0.  The stack must be quad-word aligned." A dedicated "Stack constraints at a public interface" section additionally states: "The stack must also conform to the following constraint at a public interface: - SP mod 16 = 0. The stack must be quad-word aligned." Both the universal (hardware-enforced) and public-interface constraints require 16-byte alignment, so the fact's "at all public interfaces" framing is exactly supported.

Claim 2 (frame record = two 64-bit values, FP then LR) — CONFIRMED. The "The Frame Pointer" section states: "Each frame shall link to the frame of its caller by means of a frame record of two 64-bit values on the stack (independent of the data model)... The lowest addressed double-word shall point to the previous frame record and the highest addressed double-word shall contain the value passed in LR on entry to the current function." The count is exactly "two 64-bit values," the lower address holds the previous-frame-record pointer (saved FP) and the higher address holds LR — matching the fact's "(FP,LR)" ordering.

The SOURCE quote 'SP mod 16 = 0. The stack must be quad-word aligned' matches the document verbatim (the Universal stack constraints section uses two spaces after the period: "= 0.  The stack"; the public-interface section uses one space — a trivial whitespace difference, substance identical). Register names, counts (two 64-bit values), and field ordering (FP low / LR high) are all exact.

Verified against ARM-software/abi-aa AAPCS64 (raw RST: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ARM-software/abi-aa/main/aapcs64/aapcs64.rst).

(1) Alignment — both the universal and public-interface scopes confirm 16-byte alignment verbatim:
- "Universal stack constraints" (line 1232): "SP mod 16 = 0.  The stack must be quad-word aligned." (preceded by line 1230: "at any point at which memory is accessed via SP, the hardware requires that").
- "Stack constraints at a public interface" (lines 1237-1239): "The stack must also conform to the following constraint at a public interface: - SP mod 16 = 0. The stack must be quad-word aligned."
This exactly supports "SP mod 16 = 0 (quad-word aligned) at all public interfaces."

(2) Frame record — "The Frame Pointer" section (line 1244): "Each frame shall link to the frame of its caller by means of a frame record of two 64-bit values on the stack (independent of the data model). The frame record for the innermost frame ... shall be pointed to by the frame pointer register (FP). The lowest addressed double-word shall point to the previous frame record and the highest addressed double-word shall contain the value passed in LR on entry to the current function." This confirms the frame record is two 64-bit values on the stack: saved/previous frame pointer (FP) at the lower address and LR at the higher address — i.e. (FP, LR).

The cited SOURCE quote ("Universal stack constraints: 'SP mod 16 = 0. The stack must be quad-word aligned'") matches the source verbatim, and the public-interface scope is independently supported. No version/scope overclaim detected.

fact: Naked functions were stabilized in Rust 1.88.0, released 2025-06-26: marked `#[unsafe(naked)]`, body is a single `core::arch::naked_asm!` call, compiler emits no prologue/epilogue and no arg/return handling; release note explicitly names operating systems as the use-case. naked_asm! is only allowed inside naked functions; asm!/global_asm! are the other two macros.
source: Rust 1.88.0 announcement blog (blog.rust-lang.org/2025/06/26/Rust-1.88.0); Rust reference Inline assembly chapter
evidence:
Every sub-claim is exactly supported by the two fetched authoritative sources.

1) Version + date + stabilization. Rust 1.88.0 blog (blog.rust-lang.org/2025/06/26/Rust-1.88.0), byline "June 26, 2025 - The Rust Release Team", title "Announcing Rust 1.88.0", has a dedicated "Naked functions" section: "Rust now supports writing naked functions with no compiler-generated epilogue and prologue, allowing full control over the generated assembly for a particular function." CONFIRMS stabilized in 1.88.0, released 2025-06-26, and "no prologue/epilogue".

2) Attribute name. "A naked function is marked with the #[unsafe(naked)] attribute, and its body consists of a single naked_asm! call." CONFIRMS the exact `#[unsafe(naked)]` spelling and "body is a single naked_asm! call". The blog example uses `core::arch::naked_asm!(...)`.

3) No arg/return handling. "The handwritten assembly block defines the entire function body: unlike non-naked functions, the compiler does not add any special handling for arguments or return values." CONFIRMS "no arg/return handling".

4) OS use-case named in the release note. "Naked functions are used in low-level settings like Rust's compiler-builtins, operating systems, and embedded applications." CONFIRMS "release note explicitly names operating systems as the use-case".

5) naked_asm! only inside naked functions. Rust reference Inline assembly chapter (doc.rust-lang.org/reference/inline-assembly.html): "With the naked_asm! macro, the assembly code is emitted in a function scope and constitutes the full assembly code of a function. The naked_asm! macro is only allowed in naked functions." CONFIRMS.

6) The three macros / other two are asm! and global_asm!. Reference: "Support for inline assembly is provided via the asm!, naked_asm!, and global_asm! macros." and "Inline assembly can be used in one of three ways." CONFIRMS asm! and global_asm! are the other two macros.

Register-and-field-exactness check (the lens): the blog's worked example is internally consistent with the SysV AMD64 ABI it declares (`extern "sysv64"`): `fn wrapping_add(a: u64, b: u64) -> u64` with body `lea rax, [rdi + rsi]` / `ret` -- args a,b arrive in rdi,rsi and the u64 return is in rax, exactly per the System V AMD64 integer arg/return register assignment. No register name, macro count (3), or attribute field is misstated.

All seven sub-claims are confirmed verbatim against the two cited authoritative sources fetched directly.

SOURCE 1 — Rust 1.88.0 announcement blog (https://blog.rust-lang.org/2025/06/26/Rust-1.88.0/), title "Announcing Rust 1.88.0 | Rust Blog", dated "June 26, 2025 · The Rust Release Team". This confirms version 1.88.0 and release date 2025-06-26.

Under the "Naked functions" section the blog states:
- "Rust now supports writing naked functions with no compiler-generated epilogue and prologue, allowing full control over the generated assembly for a particular function." (confirms no prologue/epilogue)
- "A naked function is marked with the #[unsafe(naked)] attribute, and its body consists of a single naked_asm! call." (confirms #[unsafe(naked)] attribute + single naked_asm! body)
- Example shows the body as a single `core::arch::naked_asm!( "lea rax, [rdi + rsi]", "ret" );` call.
- "The handwritten assembly block defines the entire function body: unlike non-naked functions, the compiler does not add any special handling for arguments or return values." (confirms no arg/return handling)
- "Naked functions are used in low-level settings like Rust's compiler-builtins, operating systems, and embedded applications." (confirms the release note explicitly names operating systems as a use-case)

SOURCE 2 — Rust Reference, Inline assembly chapter (https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/inline-assembly.html):
- "Support for inline assembly is provided via the asm!, naked_asm!, and global_asm! macros." (confirms asm! and global_asm! are the other two of the three macros)
- "Inline assembly can be used in one of three ways."
- "With the naked_asm! macro, the assembly code is emitted in a function scope and constitutes the full assembly code of a function. The naked_asm! macro is only allowed in naked functions." (confirms naked_asm! is only allowed inside naked functions)

Version/scope exactness: version 1.88.0 exact, date 2025-06-26 exact, attribute spelling #[unsafe(naked)] exact, the three-macro set (asm!/naked_asm!/global_asm!) exact, and the "operating systems" use-case naming is literally present. No overclaim detected.
